records.ety: [OE. sete, Icel. saeti; akin to Sw. säte, Dan. saede, MHG. sāze, AS. set, setl, and E. sit. √154. See Sit, and cf. Settle, n.]

defs: The place or thing upon which one sits; hence; anything made to be sat in or upon, as a chair, bench, stool, saddle, or the like.
quotes: And Jesus . . . overthrew the tables of the money changers, and the seats of them that sold doves.

defs: The place occupied by anything, or where any person or thing is situated, resides, or abides; a site; an abode, a station; a post; a situation.
quotes: Where thou dwellest, even where Satan's seat is.
He that builds a fair house upon an ill seat committeth himself to prison.
A seat of plenty, content, and tranquillity.

defs: That part of a thing on which a person sits; as, the seat of a chair or saddle; the seat of a pair of pantaloons.
A sitting; a right to sit; regular or appropriate place of sitting; as, a seat in a church; a seat for the season in the opera house.
Posture, or way of sitting, on horseback.
quotes: She had so good a seat and hand she might be trusted with any mount.

defs: A part or surface on which another part or surface rests; as, a valve seat.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Seated; p. pr. & vb. n. Seating.]

defs: To place on a seat; to cause to sit down; as, to seat one's self.
quotes: The guests were no sooner seated but they entered into a warm debate.

defs: To cause to occupy a post, site, situation, or the like; to station; to establish; to fix; to settle.
quotes: Thus high . . . is King Richard seated.
They had seated themselves in New Guiana.

defs: To assign a seat to, or the seats of; to give a sitting to; as, to seat a church, or persons in a church.

quotes: From their foundations, loosening to and fro, They plucked the seated hills.

defs: To settle; to plant with inhabitants; as to seat a country.
To put a seat or bottom in; as, to seat a chair.
